danger_signals.visual_form: Different unit sprites representing rival civilizations' military forces displayed on isometric tile map
danger_signals.meaning: Military threat; potential for conflict or conquest

danger_signals.visual_form: Non-playable character unit sprites distinct from player and AI civilization units
danger_signals.meaning: Uncontrolled hostile entities; random danger to settlements and trade routes

danger_signals.visual_form: Diplomatic status change (inferred from diplomatic relations system)
danger_signals.meaning: Civilization has declared hostile intent; cities are now targetable

danger_signals.visual_form: Fog of war concealing unexplored map tiles from player view; only immediate unit vicinity visible
danger_signals.meaning: Unknown threats; potential enemy positions or barbarian settlements beyond sight

safety_signals.visual_form: Diplomatic status indicator showing non-aggression pact or peaceful relations
safety_signals.meaning: No immediate military threat from this civilization

safety_signals.visual_form: Cities labeled with text on tiles; friendly units displayed in player's color scheme
safety_signals.meaning: Controlled territory and forces; safe zones for resource gathering

safety_signals.visual_form: Tiles showing irrigation and other improvements on established cities' surrounding tiles
safety_signals.meaning: Developed, controlled land providing food, trade, and production yields

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Unit sprites rendered in civilization-specific visual style (inferred from historical civilization selection mechanic)
Distinct NPC unit sprites not aligned with any playable civilization
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Uncontrolled territory; areas without city development

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Limited unit types available (settlers, basic military); large unexplored map areas shrouded in fog of war; few cities on map
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player and AI focus on exploration and city founding; limited unit movement and action options

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increased diversity of unit types displayed (engineers, improved military units); more developed terrain with irrigation and improvements visible; expanding city networks
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Active diplomatic relations; trade and diplomacy become viable victory paths; increased military unit deployment

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Late game - advanced civilization (modern era to 2020 AD)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Advanced unit types (cruiser ships visible in screenshot); spaceship construction visible; highly developed map with widespread improvements; wonders of the world constructed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Technological dominance allows multiple victory paths; spaceship construction becomes visible goal; military and diplomatic balance shifts

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy unit presence increases on map; hostile units positioned near player cities; potential siege formations visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Units engage in combat; city conquest occurs; diplomatic reputation system reflects hostility

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy units withdraw or cease aggressive positioning; diplomatic status UI changes to reflect non-aggression
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Military operations cease; trade and diplomatic relations resume; units may pass through or near opponent territory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Isometric tile map displays different terrain types (grassland, mountain, water, etc.); special resources marked on tiles; improvements like irrigation show resource development
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Cities harvest yields (food, trade, production) from adjacent tiles; unit movement restricted by terrain; water units confined to water tiles

environmental_storytelling.example: Different tile types (mountains, forests, grasslands, water) visible on isometric map communicate natural world variation; special resources indicate strategic locations for city placement and expansion
Irrigation shown on tiles surrounding cities indicates productive, controlled land; absence of improvements on distant tiles suggests frontier or barbarian-controlled regions

environmental_storytelling.technique: Unit type diversity as technological progress indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: Early game shows only settlers and primitive military units; advanced eras display specialized units (engineers, cruiser ships) that visually communicate technological advancement and civilization development
Unexplored tiles obscured from player view; revealed tiles show terrain and existing civilizations; visual contrast between known and unknown territory creates sense of frontier and discovery
Dense city clustering with improvements indicates developed regions; sparse settlements suggest frontier or lower-resource areas; visual layout communicates civilization expansion strategy
Special buildable structures serve as visual monuments to technological and cultural achievement; presence of wonders on map indicates advanced civilizations

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Stationary on single tile; surrounded by improved tiles with irrigation; generate resource yields visually represented
Positioned adjacent to or within striking range of enemy cities; move toward enemy territory; engage in visible battle animations
Positioned around own cities; garrison within city tiles; remain stationary near borders
Move across map; pause at selected locations to found new cities; visible on tiles before city construction
Unpredictable movement; attack nearby player or AI units; spawn in unexplored or uncontrolled territory; visual distinction from faction-controlled units
Visible on map; move to adjacent tiles; construct improvements (irrigation) that visually change tile appearance
Confined to water tiles; move through water; demonstrate advanced technology; visible in advanced game eras
Units visible on map; cities established; diplomatic reputation system shows attitude (hostile, neutral, friendly) through status indicators

notes: Civilization II uses an isometric tile-based representation of the world map, upgraded from the original game's top-down view. Faction identity relies primarily on civilization selection (21 historical civilizations) and unit visual differentiation rather than color coding or distinct sigils (not documented in source). World state is communicated through: (1) visible unit diversity reflecting technological era, (2) map development density indicating civilization advancement, (3) fog of war revealing explored vs. unexplored territory, (4) diplomatic status changes indicating peace/war, and (5) terrain and improvement visibility showing resource control. The game's manifestation layer emphasizes territorial control and technological progression as primary world-state communicators. Barbarians serve as environmental danger signal distinct from faction-controlled threats. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on tile-state visualization and unit placement patterns rather than narrative or symbolic decoration.
